attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with a two-year history of progressively worsening heavy menstrual bleeding. She reports changing pads every two hours during her heaviest days and passing large clots. She has completed her family with three children all born with vaginal deliveries. Her medical history includes iron deficiency anaemia. She takes oral iron supplementation.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2. Abdominal examination is unremarkable. Recent investigations show haemoglobin 95 g/L (115-165), ferritin 15 µg/L (20-200). Transvaginal ultrasound shows normal endometrial thickness and no fibroids. Endometrial pipelle shows proliferative endometrium only. She is interested in endometrial ablation as a definitive treatment.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Endometrial ablation with laparoscopic bilateral salpingectomy
Endometrial ablation with combined oral contraceptive pill
Endometrial ablation with copper intrauterine device
attributes.explanation:
This patient has heavy menstrual bleeding causing iron deficiency anaemia that has been appropriately investigated with ultrasound and endometrial sampling. When performing endometrial ablation, concurrent permanent contraception is essential because pregnancy after ablation carries significant risks including uterine rupture, placental adherence disorders, and severe intrauterine growth restriction.

Endometrial ablation with laparoscopic bilateral salpingectomy is the most appropriate management as it provides both definitive treatment for heavy menstrual bleeding and permanent contraception. Current evidence supports bilateral salpingectomy over traditional tubal ligation as the preferred method of surgical sterilisation, as it may reduce the risk of epithelial ovarian cancer, which is now thought to frequently originate in the fallopian tubes.

Endometrial ablation with progesterone-only pill is inadequate as it relies on patient compliance and does not provide permanent contraception. While the progesterone-only pill can help manage heavy menstrual bleeding, it is not appropriate in this context as reversible contraception is insufficient protection after ablation.

Endometrial ablation alone is contraindicated as pregnancy after ablation, while rare, can occur if no permanent contraception is in place. The partially destroyed endometrium can still support implantation but cannot support normal placentation, leading to potentially catastrophic pregnancy complications.

Endometrial ablation with combined oral contraceptive pill is unsuitable as it does not provide permanent contraception. Additionally, ongoing hormonal therapy may mask post-ablation bleeding that could signal complications or treatment failure.

Endometrial ablation with copper intrauterine device is not appropriate as the device could interfere with the ablation procedure and does not provide permanent contraception. Copper intrauterine devices can also be associated with increased risk for heavy menstrual bleeding.
learningPoints: Endometrial ablation requires concurrent permanent contraception due to risk of pregnancy complications.
Bilateral salpingectomy is preferred over tubal ligation due to reduced ovarian cancer risk.
Heavy menstrual bleeding requires investigation with full blood count and pelvic ultrasound before definitive management.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Endometrial Ablation [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2022 [cited 2024 Nov 30]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Endometrial-Ablation.pdf

Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Heavy Menstrual Bleeding [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2021 [cited 2024 Nov 30]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Heavy-Menstrual-Bleeding.pdf
